_meta.authoringNotes: Content cross-verified against 2022 American Academy of Orthopaedic Surgeons (AAOS) shoulder instability guidelines, 2020 American Shoulder and Elbow Surgeons (ASES) SLAP consensus statement, and Burke et al. 2021 systematic review on SLAP repair outcomes. ICD-10 code S43.4 confirmed against CMS ICD-10-CM 2026. Physical examination maneuvers referenced from Hegedus 2012 systematic review.

h1Title: Labral Tear of the Shoulder: SLAP Tear, Bankart Lesion, and Treatment Options
heroOverview: A labral tear of the shoulder refers to injury to the fibrocartilaginous ring (the glenoid labrum) that deepens the shoulder socket and serves as an attachment site for the biceps tendon. It typically results from either acute trauma — such as a fall on an outstretched arm or shoulder dislocation — or repetitive overhead activity seen in throwers, swimmers, and weightlifters. The injury causes deep shoulder pain, a sensation of catching or locking, and reduced throwing velocity. Diagnosis is confirmed by specific physical examination maneuvers and MRI arthrogram, and treatment ranges from physical therapy to arthroscopic repair depending on tear type and patient goals.

keyStats.prevalence: SLAP tears account for 4-6% of all shoulder injuries and 26% of shoulder injuries in overhead athletes
keyStats.demographics: Most common in males aged 20-40; highest incidence in contact and overhead sports
keyStats.avgAge: Peak incidence in the third and fourth decades of life
keyStats.globalCases: Incidence estimated at 3.8 per 10,000 person-years in the general population, higher in athletic cohorts

definition: A labral tear (ICD-10: S43.4) is an injury to the glenoid labrum — the fibrocartilaginous rim surrounding the glenoid socket of the scapula. The labrum increases socket depth by roughly 50%, provides attachment for the glenohumeral ligaments, and stabilizes the humeral head against translation. Labral tears are classified by location and mechanism: SLAP (Superior Labrum Anterior and Posterior) tears involve the upper quadrant where the long head of biceps tendon anchors; Bankart lesions affect the anterior-inferior labrum and are the primary structural injury in traumatic shoulder dislocations; and posterior labral tears involve the back of the socket. These injuries disrupt the passive and active stabilizers of the glenohumeral joint, leading to pain, mechanical symptoms (catching, popping, locking), and instability. The SLAP classification system (Snyder 1990) grades tears I through IV based on anatomy and severity. Management spans physical therapy for most lower-grade tears to arthroscopic repair or reconstruction for higher-grade or symptomatic injuries, with outcomes heavily influenced by tear type, patient age, and activity demands.

typesClassification.description: Wear and fraying of the superior labrum without detachment. A degenerative change seen in overhead athletes and older adults. Often asymptomatic and managed conservatively.

The most clinically significant SLAP tear. The biceps tendon anchor separates from the superior glenoid, disrupting stability. Occurs in throwing athletes (peel-back mechanism) and after acute traction injuries. Surgical repair is often required.

A vertical bucket-handle tear of the superior labrum with an intact biceps anchor. The torn fragment can displace into the joint, causing mechanical symptoms. Debridement or repair depending on fragment size.

typesClassification.type: SLAP IV — bucket-handle tear with biceps involvement
typesClassification.description: A bucket-handle tear extending into the biceps tendon. The biceps anchor is partially attached. Outcomes are worse than SLAP II — treatment depends on the degree of biceps involvement and patient demands.

typesClassification.type: Bankart lesion — anterior inferior labral tear
typesClassification.description: Detachment of the antero-inferior glenoid labrum, typically from a traumatic anterior shoulder dislocation. The hallmark of traumatic anterior instability. Absent bony involvement (soft tissue Bankart), it is repaired arthroscopically with high success rates.
Involves the posterior glenoid labrum. Associated with posterior shoulder dislocations, seizures, and repetitive posterior loading in football linemen. Less common than anterior lesions.

primarySymptoms: Deep, poorly localized shoulder pain — often described as aching in the posterior deltoid region — that worsens with overhead activity, especially in the cocking phase of throwing.
A sensation of catching, popping, or locking within the shoulder joint, particularly when rotating the arm upward or reaching behind the back.
Feelings of shoulder instability or 'looseness,' as if the shoulder might slip out of joint during certain arm positions or activities.
Weakness in shoulder rotation and elevation, particularly with overhead reaching, throwing, or lifting objects away from the body.
Reduced throwing velocity in throwers, with difficulty achieving the late cocking phase due to pain and loss of posterior-superior stability.
Pain with specific physical examination maneuvers — active compression (O'Brien test), relocation test, and anterior slide test — that reproduce mechanical symptoms.
Pain radiating down the upper arm, occasionally extending past the elbow, consistent with referral from the glenohumeral joint.
Shoulder stiffness and limited range of motion in adhesive capsulitis that develops as a secondary consequence of pain-avoidance behavior.
A audible click or clunk on certain arm positions, particularly during combined elevation and external rotation — a sign of labral fragement displacement.
Night pain, especially when lying on the affected shoulder or sleeping with the arm elevated, which disrupts sleep in active patients.
earlyWarningSigns: Transient shoulder pain or 'zinger' sensations during the late cocking phase of throwing that resolves shortly after stopping activity.
Noticing a subtle reduction in throwing velocity or accuracy without an acute cause — a common early sign in throwers with SLAP II tears.
A single previous shoulder dislocation or subluxation event followed by persistent mild shoulder discomfort that never fully resolves.
Post-operative stiffness that develops earlier than expected, with loss of external rotation more pronounced than flexion — possible sign of adhesive capsulitis requiring intensified PT.
Recurrence of mechanical symptoms (catching, popping) within weeks of returning to overhead activity after a shoulder injury, even if pain has subsided.
emergencySigns: A shoulder that appears deformed after trauma with the arm held slightly abducted and externally rotated — indicating acute dislocation requiring prompt closed reduction to prevent neurovascular compromise.
Numbness, tingling, or weakness in the hand or fingers following a shoulder injury — possible brachial plexus or axillary nerve involvement requiring urgent evaluation.
Severe pain and inability to use the arm following a seizure or electrical injury, with concern for posterior shoulder dislocation, which is frequently missed on initial evaluation.
Signs of open shoulder injury with a visible wound over the shoulder joint after high-energy trauma — possible open dislocation requiring surgical debridement and tetanus prophylaxis.
Cold, pale hand on the affected side with weak or absent radial pulse — indicating vascular compromise requiring immediate vascular surgery consultation.

causes.description: The most common mechanism for Bankart lesions. An anterior force applied to an abducted, externally rotated arm (fall on outstretched hand, tackle, contact sport) pulls the humeral head anteriorly and avulses the antero-inferior labrum from the glenoid rim. Bankart lesions occur in approximately 85% of traumatic anterior dislocations.

A fall onto the palm with the arm extended transmits force through the elbow and shoulder, loading the glenohumeral joint. The mechanism can produce SLAP tears, humeral avulsion of glenohumeral ligaments (HAGHL lesions), or a combination. Common in cycling, gymnastics, and equestrian sports.

In late cocking and early acceleration, the biceps tendon exerts a posteriorly directed force on the superior glenoid attachment. In throwers, this 'peel-back' mechanism causes progressive superior labral delamination over thousands of throws. SLAP II tears in throwers are primarily this mechanism.

Swimmers, construction workers, painters, and weightlifters repetitively load the shoulder above shoulder level. The cumulative microtrauma produces fraying and eventual SLAP I-II tears without a single identifiable acute event.

An acute strong contraction of the biceps — such as lifting a heavy object or catching a falling weight — loads the biceps anchor at the superior glenoid and can avulse the superior labrum. Less common but well-documented in weightlifting and manual labor.

Violent asymmetric muscle contraction during a generalized tonic-clonic seizure preferentially contracts the internal rotators, pulling the humeral head posteriorly. This mechanism produces reverse Bankart lesions (posterior labral tears) and is a common cause of posterior shoulder dislocations.

riskFactors.description: Males have 3-4x higher risk of traumatic shoulder instability and SLAP tears due to higher participation in contact sports and mechanism exposure.
The third and fourth decades carry peak incidence for traumatic labral tears. SLAP tears in patients over 40 are more often degenerative than traumatic and have different treatment implications.

riskFactors.factor: Participation in contact or collision sports
riskFactors.description: Football, rugby, wrestling, hockey, and martial arts carry the highest per-athlete risk for traumatic shoulder dislocation and Bankart lesions due to direct collision mechanisms.
Baseball pitchers, tennis players, volleyball players, and swimmers repetitively stress the superior labrum through the peel-back mechanism and generate the highest rates of SLAP II tears.

Each dislocation substantially raises the risk of a recurrent instability event. After a first traumatic dislocation in a young active patient, recurrence rates exceed 50% without surgical stabilization.

Painters, construction workers, warehouse staff, and mechanics who work overhead for extended periods accumulate repetitive microtrauma to the labrum. Ergonomic modifications and periodic rest reduce risk.

riskFactors.factor: Inadequate rotator cuff or scapular strength
riskFactors.description: Deficits in the rotator cuff and scapular stabilizers increase glenohumeral translation and labral loading during activity. Strengthening programs prevent the majority of overuse-related labral injuries.

Overhead pressing, pull-ups with excessive range, and behind-the-neck presses stress the superior labrum. Proper technique, controlled load progression, and avoiding end-range positions reduce labral strain.
Patients with Beighton scores of 4 or higher have inherently looser glenohumeral ligaments and higher rates of instability and labral injury. Targeted stabilization exercises reduce risk.

riskFactors.factor: Failure to rehabilitate fully after initial shoulder injury
riskFactors.description: Returning to sport before full rotator cuff and scapular control is restored substantially raises re-injury risk. Discharge criteria from physical therapy should include strength symmetry and sport-specific testing.

affectedDemographics: SLAP tears most commonly affect males aged 20-40 participating in overhead or contact sports
Bankart lesions from traumatic dislocation occur predominantly in young males under 30 — recurrence rates reach 50-90% in patients under 20 without surgical stabilization
Posterior labral tears occur in football linemen, shot-putters, and other athletes with posterior loading mechanisms
Degenerative SLAP I-II fraying is common in patients over 40 and is often an incidental finding on MRI rather than the primary pain source
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis of a labral tear begins with a detailed history and physical examination, which together form the clinical diagnosis that guides imaging. Patients typically report deep shoulder pain, mechanical symptoms (catching, locking, popping), and often a history of trauma or overhead sport. On examination, specific provocative tests assess labral integrity: the active compression test (O'Brien test) compresses the superior labrum with the arm in forward flexion, adduction, and internal rotation; the Jobe relocation test applies posterior force to the humeral head while the arm is abducted and externally rotated; the anterior slide test loads the biceps anchor anteriorly. These tests have variable sensitivity (50-80%) and specificity (50-70%) individually — combinations of two or more positive tests improve diagnostic accuracy. MRI arthrogram (MRA) with intra-articular gadolinium contrast is the imaging gold standard, with 80-90% sensitivity and 90% specificity for SLAP II tears. CT arthrogram is an alternative when MRA is contraindicated. Plain radiographs are normal in isolated labral tears but rule out associated bony Bankart or Hill-Sachs lesions. Shoulder arthroscopy is both diagnostic and therapeutic — tears identified intraoperatively are often treated in the same setting. The key clinical principle is that not every labral tear requires surgery: SLAP I fraying, degenerative tears in older patients, and isolated labral tears without instability in non-throwing patients are typically managed conservatively.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Provocative test for superior labral tears. A positive test reproduces deep shoulder pain when the arm is resisted in forward flexion, adduction, and internal rotation; pain relieved by applied external rotation suggests a SLAP tear.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A clinician or physical therapist positions the arm and applies downward resistance. Reproduction of deep pain that differs from simple impingement pain is recorded as positive. Perform bilaterally for comparison.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Assesses anterior glenohumeral instability and SLAP involvement. In the apprehension position (arm in 90 degrees abduction and external rotation), a posteriorly directed force on the humeral head reduces symptoms in instability patients.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The examiner applies a posteriorly directed force to the anterior humeral head while the patient is in the apprehension position. Relief of apprehension or pain confirms a positive test.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Gold-standard non-invasive imaging for labral tears. Intra-articular gadolinium distends the joint and tracks into labral defects, increasing sensitivity for detecting SLAP tears, Bankart lesions, and HAGHL injuries.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A radiologist injects gadolinium contrast into the shoulder joint under ultrasound or fluoroscopic guidance, followed by MRI scanning of the shoulder. The procedure takes 30-45 minutes. Mild soreness at the injection site for 24-48 hours is normal.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Preferred over MRA when bony detail is needed — for example, assessing glenoid bone loss in recurrent dislocation or Hill-Sachs lesion configuration. Less sensitive for soft-tissue-only labral tears than MRA.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Contrast is injected into the shoulder joint under fluoroscopy, followed by CT scanning. Takes 20-30 minutes. Useful for surgical planning when bone pathology is suspected.

diagnosticTests.test: Plain shoulder radiographs (AP, scapular Y, axillary lateral)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Rule out fractures, dislocations, osteoarthritis, and bony Bankart lesions. Normal in isolated soft-tissue labral tears — their role is to exclude differential diagnoses and associated bony injuries.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Two or three standard shoulder views taken standing or seated. Takes less than 5 minutes with minimal radiation.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Direct visualization of the labrum, glenohumeral ligaments, and biceps anchor with the ability to probe tissue integrity. Indicated when history, exam, and imaging are discordant, or when other intra-articular pathology is suspected.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: An orthopedic surgeon inserts a small camera through a portal into the shoulder joint under anesthesia. The procedure is both diagnostic and therapeutic — identified tears can be repaired in the same setting. Performed as day surgery.

treatmentOverview: Treatment of labral tears follows a decision tree based on tear type, patient age and activity level, symptoms, and the presence or absence of instability. Physical therapy is the cornerstone of treatment for most SLAP I-II tears without instability and for initial Bankart management — focused on rotator cuff strengthening, scapular stabilization, and neuromuscular control. A structured 3-6 month rehabilitation program achieves satisfactory outcomes in 65-75% of SLAP II tears in non-throwing patients. Arthroscopic repair is indicated for SLAP II tears that fail conservative treatment, all Bankart lesions in young active patients after dislocation, and any labral tear associated with clinically significant instability. The surgical approach uses absorbable suture anchors placed at the glenoid rim to re-attach the labrum to bone. Outcomes vary by population: arthroscopic Bankart repair for traumatic anterior instability achieves 90-95% stability rates at 5-10 years. SLAP II repair in overhead athletes is more challenging — return-to-sport rates are 60-80%, and elite throwers often cannot reach pre-injury velocity levels. Complications of surgery include adhesive capsulitis (20-30%), which is managed with intensive PT and occasionally manipulation under anesthesia; nerve injury (rare, <2%); and recurrent instability (5-15%). The critical decision is matching treatment intensity to the patient's functional demands — a laborer with instability requires different management than a sedentary office worker with a degeneratively frayed labrum.

medicalTreatments.name: Physical therapy — rotator cuff and scapular stabilization program
medicalTreatments.description: Structured exercise program targeting the rotator cuff (supraspinatus, infraspinatus, teres minor, subscapularis), serratus anterior, trapezius, and core. Protocols such as the Ham III and Andrews program use progressive resistance, proprioception, and sport-specific drills over 3-6 months.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Symptom improvement in 65-75% of non-throwing SLAP II patients over 3-6 months; less effective in throwing athletes where the peel-back mechanism persists.

medicalTreatments.name: NSAIDs (naproxen 500 mg twice daily, ibuprofen 800 mg three times daily)
medicalTreatments.description: Anti-inflammatory medications for pain management during rehabilitation. Reduce inflammation in the glenohumeral joint and allow more comfortable participation in physical therapy. Used for 2-4 weeks during acute flare.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Pain reduction in 60-70% of patients within 1-2 weeks; primarily symptomatic with no effect on tear healing.

medicalTreatments.name: Corticosteroid injection into glenohumeral joint
medicalTreatments.description: Intra-articular triamcinolone or methylprednisolone injection for pain relief in refractory cases. Reduces inflammation in the joint capsule. Useful when pain limits participation in rehabilitation.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Pain relief in 70-80% for 4-8 weeks; repeated injections are avoided due to risk of tendon weakening and cartilage degradation with more than 2-3 injections per year.

medicalTreatments.name: Activity modification — temporary reduction in aggravating activities
medicalTreatments.description: Reducing or modifying activities that provoke symptoms — particularly overhead positions, throwing, and heavy lifting — reduces labral loading and allows inflammation to settle. A period of 4-8 weeks of modification combined with PT is often sufficient for lower-grade tears.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Combined with PT, activity modification produces improvement in 60-70% of symptomatic labral tears without requiring surgical intervention.

medicalTreatments.name: Platelet-rich plasma (PRP) injection
medicalTreatments.description: Autologous blood product rich in growth factors (PDGF, TGF-beta, VEGF) injected into the labral injury site to promote healing. Used off-label for SLAP tears as an alternative to surgery.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Emerging evidence from small RCTs suggests modest benefit over exercise alone; not yet standard of care. Larger trials are ongoing.

medicalTreatments.name: Prolotherapy (dextrose intra-articular injection)
medicalTreatments.description: Injection of 15-20% dextrose solution into the labral attachment and surrounding ligamentous tissue to stimulate a mild inflammatory healing response and tighten loose stabilizers.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Limited and conflicting evidence; some case series report improvement in mechanical symptoms in 50-60% of patients with mild instability. Not a standard recommendation.

surgicalOptions.name: Arthroscopic SLAP repair (suture anchor technique)
surgicalOptions.description: The most common surgical treatment for SLAP II tears. Small absorbable suture anchors are placed at the glenoid rim and used to re-approximate the detached superior labrum and biceps anchor to bone. The peel-back test confirms a stable repair intraoperatively.
surgicalOptions.successRate: 90-95% good-to-excellent results in non-throwing patients; in overhead athletes, return to prior level of sport is 60-80%, and throwing velocity frequently does not return to pre-injury levels.

surgicalOptions.name: Arthroscopic Bankart repair with capsulolabral reattachment
surgicalOptions.description: Arthroscopic re-attachment of the Bankart lesion and associated anteroinferior capsule to the glenoid rim using suture anchors. The gold-standard surgical treatment for traumatic anterior shoulder instability in young active patients.
surgicalOptions.successRate: 90-95% stability rates at 5-10 year follow-up in patients under 25; recurrence rates increase with age over 30, significant glenoid bone loss (>20-25%), or Hill-Sachs engagement lesions.

surgicalOptions.name: Arthroscopic or open latarjet procedure
surgicalOptions.description: A bone block procedure where the coracoid process (with attached conjoined tendon) is transferred to the anterior glenoid rim. Reserved for cases with significant glenoid bone loss (>20%) or failed Bankart repair. Provides both a bone block and a dynamic muscular sling.
surgicalOptions.successRate: 95%+ stability rates even in high-risk revision cases; slightly higher complication rate (nerve injury, graft nonunion) than isolated Bankart repair.

alternativeTreatments.name: Focused shockwave therapy
alternativeTreatments.description: Extracorporeal shockwave therapy (ESWT) has been studied in small case series for SLAP tears, with reports of improved pain and function scores in 50-60% of patients. Mechanism may involve neovascularization and tissue remodeling. Not yet validated in RCTs.

alternativeTreatments.name: Biceps tenotomy or tenodesis without SLAP repair
alternativeTreatments.description: In chronic SLAP II tears in older low-demand patients (>40) who have failed conservative management, releasing the biceps tendon (tenotomy) or reattaching it to the humerus (tenodesis) reduces mechanical symptoms by removing the biceps anchor as a pain source. Simpler than repair with faster recovery but sacrifices some elbow flexion and supination strength.

whySeeSpecialist: An orthopedic shoulder surgeon or sports medicine physician should be involved when shoulder instability is present after dislocation, when physical therapy fails to improve symptoms over 3-6 months, when imaging confirms a higher-grade SLAP II, III, or IV tear, when the patient is a competitive overhead athlete, or when considering surgical intervention. Primary care and physical therapy manage most lower-grade labral tears effectively without surgical referral.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for a fellowship-trained shoulder or sports medicine orthopedic surgeon who performs at least 50-100 shoulder arthroscopy cases per year. Board certification in orthopedic surgery with subspecialty fellowship training is the baseline. Ask about the surgeon's experience with SLAP repair specifically in overhead athletes — outcomes differ substantially from general instability repair. A good surgeon will discuss the trade-offs honestly, including the risk of not returning to sport at the prior level after SLAP II repair in throwing athletes.
preventionStrategies: Maintain rotator cuff and scapular stabilizer strength throughout the year — deficits in these muscle groups are the single largest modifiable risk factor for labral injury in overhead athletes

Follow structured pitching or throwing programs that limit pitch count, enforce rest days, and incorporate pre-season conditioning — the majority of SLAP tears in throwers are preventable with appropriate load management
Use proper technique in weightlifting, particularly for overhead press and behind-the-neck movements, and avoid loading the shoulder at end-range in external rotation
Address shoulder pain promptly with a period of modified activity rather than training through pain — continuing activity on a symptomatic shoulder accelerates labral deterioration
Screen annually for shoulder hypermobility and glenohumeral strength imbalances in contact and overhead athletes, and prescribe targeted prehab exercises for those with laxity
lifestyleModifications: Reduce or modify overhead activities and throwing volume when shoulder fatigue or mild pain develops — do not train through moderate or severe shoulder pain
Sleep with the shoulder in a pain-free position; avoid lying directly on the affected shoulder when acute symptoms are present
Apply ice to the shoulder after overhead activity or when pain flares, for 15-20 minutes at a time to reduce inflammation
Use proper workstation ergonomics to avoid chronic scapular protraction, which increases glenohumeral translation and labral strain in desk workers
Incorporate daily scapular setting and thoracic mobility exercises to maintain optimal shoulder mechanics and reduce compensatory overload
Maintain general upper body strength — especially posterior shoulder chain (external rotators, lower trapezius, rhomboids) — to keep the humeral head centered in the socket during all activities

recommended: Anti-inflammatory foods (fatty fish, leafy greens, berries) — chronic low-grade inflammation can amplify pain from labral pathology
Adequate protein intake to support soft tissue healing — 1.2-1.6 g/kg/day, especially during post-injury or post-surgical rehabilitation
Vitamin D and calcium — adequate levels support bone health around the glenoid, particularly important if bony Bankart or Latarjet surgery is contemplated
Water — staying well-hydrated supports joint fluid dynamics and general tissue health
Magnesium-rich foods (nuts, legumes, whole grains) — magnesium helps with muscle relaxation and can reduce secondary muscle spasm around the shoulder girdle
avoid: Pro-inflammatory diet (excess refined sugars, processed meats, excessive alcohol) — can amplify baseline inflammation and amplify pain from a labral tear
High-sodium diet — contributes to systemic fluid retention and may increase perceived shoulder stiffness
Smoking — nicotine impairs soft tissue healing and is associated with worse outcomes after shoulder surgery
Excessive caffeine — contributes to muscle tension and can exacerbate pain-avoidance patterns in the shoulder girdle

exerciseGuidelines: Exercise is both the primary treatment for labral tears and the essential component of post-surgical rehabilitation. During conservative management, a supervised PT program is preferred for the first 6-8 weeks, progressing to independent gym-based or home exercise program once movement patterns are normalized. Post-surgical rehabilitation follows a staged protocol: passive range of motion weeks 0-4, active-assisted and gentle strengthening months 2-3, progressive strengthening months 3-6, and sport-specific training from month 6 onward. Return to throwing or overhead sport requires passing of progressive interval throwing programs and strength symmetry testing — typically 9-12 months post-surgical repair.
dailyManagement: Perform prescribed rotator cuff and scapular exercises 5-6 days per week during the active rehabilitation phase — consistency drives outcomes more than any specific exercise choice
Apply ice for 15-20 minutes after PT sessions or any aggravating activity to manage inflammation
Avoid sleeping on the affected shoulder for the first 4-6 weeks after injury or post-surgery; use a pillow for arm support when lying on the unaffected side
Track pain levels and function using a simple scale (0-10 at rest, 0-10 with activity) to monitor progress and detect setbacks early
Communicate with your physical therapist or surgeon if range of motion regresses or pain increases — early intervention for stiffness is far more effective than late intervention
prognosis: The prognosis of a labral tear varies substantially based on tear type, patient age, activity demands, and treatment. Physical therapy achieves good-to-excellent outcomes in 65-75% of SLAP II tears in non-throwing patients over 3-6 months. Arthroscopic SLAP II repair in non-throwing patients restores stability and function in 90-95%, though overhead athletes face a more uncertain course — 20-40% of elite throwers do not return to their prior level of sport. Bankart repair for traumatic anterior instability in patients under 25 achieves 90-95% stability at 5-10 years, making surgery the preferred option in this group rather than repeated dislocation episodes. Posterior labral tears have more variable outcomes due to diagnostic delays and less well-defined treatment algorithms. Complications — adhesive capsulitis, persistent instability, and re-tear — occur in 10-20% of surgically repaired patients and are more common when rehabilitation is incomplete. Without treatment, symptomatic labral tears do not spontaneously heal: instability tends to worsen over time, and recurrent dislocations cause progressive glenoid bone loss that complicates eventual surgical management.
recoveryTimeline: Non-surgical: initial pain reduction within 2-4 weeks of PT; meaningful functional improvement by 6-8 weeks; full plateau by 3-6 months. Post-arthroscopic SLAP repair: shoulder immobilizer for 4 weeks; passive range of motion weeks 0-4; active-assisted range of motion weeks 4-8; strengthening begins at 8-10 weeks; light throwing program at 4-6 months; full return to overhead sport at 9-12 months. Post-arthroscopic Bankart repair: immobilizer for 2-4 weeks; progression similar to SLAP repair; return to contact sport at 6-9 months. Post-Latarjet: slightly longer immobilization (3-4 weeks); bone graft incorporation requires 8-10 weeks before aggressive rehabilitation; return to full contact sport at 6-9 months.
complications: Adhesive capsulitis (frozen shoulder) — the most common complication after shoulder surgery, occurring in 20-30% of patients, requiring intensive PT and occasionally manipulation under anesthesia

Recurrent shoulder instability — recurrence rates of 5-15% after Bankart repair, higher in contact athletes, patients under 25, and those with significant glenoid bone loss not addressed at the time of initial surgery
Persistent postoperative stiffness — loss of external rotation more common than flexion loss; typically responds to PT but can require arthroscopic capsular release in refractory cases

Re-tear or failure of labral repair — tears can recur after surgery, particularly with premature return to sport or in patients with residual bone loss; revision surgery has lower success rates than primary repair

Chondral injury from recurrent instability episodes — each dislocation event causes some impaction of the humeral head against the glenoid rim, producing Hill-Sachs lesions and glenoid cartilage damage that accumulates over multiple episodes

supportResources.name: American Academy of Orthopaedic Surgeons — Shoulder Instability
supportResources.description: Evidence-based patient information on shoulder instability, dislocation, and labral injuries from the primary orthopedic professional organization in the United States.

supportResources.name: American Shoulder and Elbow Surgeons (ASES)
supportResources.description: Patient education resources from the leading shoulder and elbow surgical society, including information on SLAP tears and Bankart lesions.

supportResources.name: STOP Sports Injuries — Shoulder Injuries
supportResources.description: Sports injury prevention resources specifically for young athletes, with shoulder-specific content on overuse and traumatic shoulder injuries.

confusedWithConditions.name: Rotator Cuff Tear
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Rotator cuff tears produce weakness on specific manual muscle testing (especially supraspinatus and external rotation) and pain with mid-range elevation against resistance. Labral tears produce mechanical symptoms (catching, locking, popping) and pain with specific provocative positions rather than pure weakness. MRI or MRA distinguishes the two — cuff tears are visible as discontinuity of tendon fibers.

confusedWithConditions.name: Subacromial Impingement Syndrome
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Impingement causes anterolateral shoulder pain with overhead activities, positive Neer and Hawkins-Kennedy impingement signs, and is associated with subacromial bursal thickening visible on ultrasound. Labral tears cause mechanical symptoms (catching, popping) and pain at end-range positions in external rotation and abduction. The two can coexist, and both require addressing.

confusedWithConditions.name: Adhesive Capsulitis (Frozen Shoulder)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Frozen shoulder presents with global restriction of both active and passive range of motion in all planes — especially external rotation — without mechanical symptoms or instability. Labral tears allow full range of motion with mechanical symptoms at end-range and specific provocative test patterns. Frozen shoulder is more common in diabetics and adults aged 40-60; labral tears peak in the third and fourth decades.

confusedWithConditions.name: Acromioclavicular (AC) Joint Injury
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: AC joint pathology produces localized pain at the top of the shoulder over the AC joint itself, pain with cross-body adduction, and point tenderness at the joint. Labral tears cause deep shoulder pain, mechanical symptoms, and instability with a normal AC joint exam. Plain radiographs and MRA distinguish the two.

relatedConditions.name: Rotator Cuff Tear
relatedConditions.relevance: Coexists in 20-30% of patients over 40 with symptomatic shoulder complaints — both structures are stressed during overhead activity and age-related degeneration. Treatment sequencing must address both.

relatedConditions.name: Subacromial Impingement Syndrome
relatedConditions.relevance: Overhead activity that produces labral strain frequently produces subacromial impingement simultaneously. The two share a biomechanical pathway through scapular dyskinesis and rotator cuff fatigue.

relatedConditions.name: Adhesive Capsulitis
relatedConditions.relevance: Pain-avoidance behavior following labral injury frequently leads to shoulder immobilization and subsequent capsular contracture. Post-surgical adhesive capsulitis is among the most common complications.

relatedConditions.name: Shoulder Dislocation (Traumatic)
relatedConditions.relevance: Traumatic shoulder dislocation is the primary cause of Bankart lesions. Recurrent dislocation and instability are the direct clinical consequence of an incompletely healed Bankart lesion.

faqs.question: What is the glenoid labrum and what does it do?
faqs.answer: The glenoid labrum is a fibrocartilaginous rim of tissue attached to the edge of the glenoid socket of the shoulder blade. It deepens the socket by roughly 50%, provides attachment for the glenohumeral ligaments that stabilize the joint, and acts as a bumper to prevent humeral head translation. When it tears, the shoulder's stability is compromised and mechanical symptoms arise.

faqs.question: How serious is a labral tear of the shoulder?
faqs.answer: It depends on the tear type and the patient's goals. SLAP I fraying and small Bankart lesions in low-demand patients are often managed successfully with physical therapy. SLAP II tears in throwers and Bankart lesions causing instability in young active patients are more serious and often require surgical repair. The key risks of an untreated labral tear are chronic mechanical symptoms, recurrent instability, progressive glenoid bone loss from repeated dislocations, and, in throwers, career-affecting loss of performance.

faqs.question: What is a SLAP tear?
faqs.answer: SLAP stands for Superior Labrum Anterior and Posterior — a tear of the upper portion of the glenoid labrum where the long head of the biceps tendon attaches. The Snyder classification divides SLAP tears into four types: I is fraying, II is a detached biceps anchor (the most common clinically significant type), III is a bucket-handle tear with intact anchor, and IV extends into the biceps tendon.

faqs.question: What is a Bankart lesion?
faqs.answer: A Bankart lesion is a tear of the anteroinferior glenoid labrum, most commonly caused by a traumatic anterior shoulder dislocation. It is the hallmark structural injury in traumatic anterior instability. When the labrum detaches from the glenoid rim, the shoulder becomes predisposed to recurrent dislocations. It is repaired arthroscopically using suture anchors placed at the glenoid rim.

faqs.question: How is a labral tear diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis starts with history (mechanical symptoms, trauma history) and physical examination (O'Brien test, Jobe relocation test, anterior slide test). If examination is suggestive, MRI arthrogram (MRA) with intra-articular gadolinium is ordered — it is the imaging gold standard with 80-90% sensitivity for labral tears. Plain X-rays are obtained to rule out associated fractures or bone loss. Definitive diagnosis can also be made at the time of shoulder arthroscopy.

faqs.question: Can a labral tear heal without surgery?
faqs.answer: Many labral tears — particularly SLAP I and II tears without significant instability in non-throwing patients — respond to physical therapy and do not require surgery. The rotator cuff and scapular strengthening program allows the dynamic stabilizers to compensate for the damaged static stabilizer. However, a detached Bankart lesion in a young active patient and a symptomatic SLAP II tear in a throwing athlete do not reliably heal without surgical repair.

faqs.question: How long does recovery from labral tear surgery take?
faqs.answer: Recovery from arthroscopic labral repair follows a staged timeline: 0-4 weeks in a shoulder immobilizer with passive motion only; 4-8 weeks active-assisted range of motion; 8-12 weeks strengthening begins; 4-6 months light overhead activity; 6-9 months return to full overhead sport. Full recovery — including return to contact and throwing sports — typically requires 9-12 months. The first 6 weeks are critical for protecting the repair; rushing rehabilitation is the most common cause of surgical failure.

faqs.question: Will I get back to sports after labral tear surgery?
faqs.answer: For Bankart repair, 90-95% of young active patients return to their prior sport at the same level. For SLAP II repair, outcomes differ by population: 90-95% return in non-throwing patients; in overhead athletes and throwers, 60-80% return to sport and throwing velocity frequently does not return to pre-injury levels. The peel-back mechanism in throwing puts ongoing stress on the repaired superior labrum, making this a more challenging injury in throwers.

faqs.question: What is the success rate for SLAP tear surgery?
faqs.answer: Arthroscopic SLAP II repair achieves good-to-excellent results in 85-95% of non-throwing patients at 2-5 year follow-up. In overhead athletes, results are more modest: return to prior sport level is achieved in 60-80%, with lower rates in elite throwers. SLAP IV repairs have inferior outcomes to SLAP II due to biceps tendon involvement and are often treated with biceps tenodesis rather than repair.

faqs.question: What is the success rate for Bankart repair surgery?
faqs.answer: Arthroscopic Bankart repair for traumatic anterior instability achieves stability rates of 90-95% at 5-10 year follow-up in patients under 25. Recurrence rates are higher in contact athletes, patients with generalized ligamentous laxity, those with significant glenoid bone loss, and those who return to high-risk sport before 6 months. The Latarjet procedure is reserved for cases with glenoid bone loss exceeding 20-25% and achieves 95%+ stability even in revision cases.

faqs.question: What happens if a Bankart lesion is not repaired?
faqs.answer: Without surgical repair, Bankart lesions do not reliably heal. The shoulder remains predisposed to recurrent dislocation — recurrence rates after the first traumatic dislocation in patients under 25 exceed 50%. Each recurrence causes additional glenoid bone loss (a Bankart fracture or Hill-Sachs lesion), making eventual surgical stabilization more complex. In patients who do not wish to return to high-risk activities, non-operative management with aggressive PT is a reasonable alternative, but the patient must accept ongoing instability risk.

faqs.question: Can a labral tear cause chronic pain?
faqs.answer: Yes — an untreated or mistreated labral tear can cause persistent deep shoulder pain that worsens with overhead activity and interferes with sleep. Chronic pain develops through several mechanisms: persistent mechanical irritation from the torn labrum, secondary adhesive capsulitis from pain-avoidance immobility, rotator cuff overuse from compensatory patterns, and, in some cases, associated nerve sensitivity. Addressing the underlying labral pathology (through PT or surgery) is the most effective way to resolve chronic shoulder pain from this cause.

faqs.question: Can you lift weights with a labral tear?
faqs.answer: Light upper body strength training within pain limits is generally acceptable during conservative treatment for a labral tear, focusing on exercises that do not load the superior labrum — this means avoiding behind-the-neck presses, deep pull-ups, and end-range external rotation. Heavy lifting and exercises that produce mechanical symptoms should be deferred until rehabilitation restores adequate dynamic stability. After surgical repair, lifting restrictions follow a staged protocol outlined by the surgeon, typically with no lifting exceeding 1-2 lbs for the first 4-6 weeks.

faqs.question: Does a labral tear cause shoulder instability?
faqs.answer: It depends on the tear location and type. SLAP II tears alone do not typically produce gross instability but can cause subtle feelings of looseness and loss of throwing performance. Bankart lesions directly compromise the primary anterior stabilizers of the shoulder and are the most common cause of traumatic anterior instability. Posterior labral tears cause posterior instability. Any labral tear that is part of a multi-directional instability pattern requires a broader rehabilitation approach than an isolated lesion.

faqs.question: What is the difference between a SLAP tear and a Bankart lesion?
faqs.answer: A SLAP tear involves the superior labrum at the biceps anchor — the top of the glenoid socket. A Bankart lesion involves the anteroinferior labrum — the front-bottom of the socket. SLAP tears result primarily from repetitive overhead activity (peel-back mechanism) or traction injuries; Bankart lesions result from traumatic anterior dislocation. Bankart lesions cause instability; SLAP tears cause pain and mechanical symptoms. Both are diagnosed by MRA and treated by arthroscopic repair when conservative management fails.

faqs.question: Can a labral tear come back after surgery?
faqs.answer: Yes — re-tears occur in 5-15% of Bankart repairs and in a higher percentage of SLAP repairs, particularly in overhead athletes. Risk factors include premature return to sport, failure to address glenoid bone loss at the time of initial surgery, generalized ligamentous laxity, and incomplete rehabilitation. Revision surgery has lower success rates than primary repair. Adhering to the rehabilitation timeline and strength testing before return to sport substantially reduces re-tear risk.

faqs.question: Is a labral tear the same as a dislocated shoulder?
faqs.answer: No — a dislocated shoulder is an acute event in which the humeral head comes completely out of the glenoid socket. A labral tear is a structural injury to the soft tissue rim of the socket. These are related but distinct: a Bankart lesion is the labral tear that results from a shoulder dislocation, and the dislocation causes the tear. The dislocation is the event; the labral tear is the structural damage left behind. Recurrent instability after dislocation results from the incompletely healed Bankart lesion.

faqs.question: What does it feel like to have a labral tear?
faqs.answer: Most patients with a labral tear describe deep, poorly localized shoulder pain that is difficult to point to with one finger. Mechanical symptoms are characteristic: a catching, popping, or locking sensation during arm movement — particularly when reaching overhead, behind the back, or during the cocking phase of throwing. Some patients feel a sense of the shoulder slipping or being loose in certain arm positions. Pain at rest is less common than pain with activity; night pain when lying on the affected shoulder is frequent.

faqs.question: Can physical therapy fix a labral tear?
faqs.answer: Physical therapy is the first-line treatment for most labral tears and achieves satisfactory outcomes in 65-75% of SLAP II tears without instability. A well-designed program strengthens the rotator cuff, scapular stabilizers, and core to compensate for the damaged static stabilizer. The key is patient selection: physical therapy works best in patients without gross instability, without high-grade SLAP tears, and who are not elite throwers. Structured PT over 3-6 months with a qualified sports physical therapist is the standard of care.

faqs.question: When should I see a shoulder surgeon for a labral tear?
faqs.answer: See a shoulder surgeon if you have had a traumatic shoulder dislocation (particularly if you are under 30 and active), if physical therapy has not improved symptoms over 3-6 months, if you have recurrent episodes of the shoulder slipping or feeling loose (subluxations), if you are a competitive overhead athlete with persistent pain and loss of performance, or if MRI confirms a higher-grade SLAP II or III tear. Early surgical referral after dislocation in a young patient — rather than waiting for multiple recurrences — produces the best long-term stability outcomes.

faqs.question: What is a Latarjet procedure and when is it needed?
faqs.answer: The Latarjet procedure is a surgical stabilization technique in which the coracoid process (a projecting piece of bone from the scapula) is transferred to the front of the glenoid rim, secured with screws. It is used when there is significant bone loss on the glenoid face — typically more than 20-25% — such that a soft-tissue Bankart repair alone would leave insufficient bony support and recurrence would be likely. The procedure also provides a dynamic muscular sling from the transferred conjoined tendon. It achieves 95%+ stability even in revision cases.

metaTitle: Labral Tear Shoulder: SLAP, Bankart, Treatment
metaDescription: A torn shoulder labrum causes pain, instability, and catching with movement. Learn about SLAP tears, Bankart lesions, surgery options, and rehabilitation.

images.altText: Anatomical diagram showing a side view of the shoulder joint with glenoid labrum labeled
images.caption: The glenoid labrum — the fibrocartilaginous rim deepening the shoulder socket — is the structure affected in labral tears.

images.altText: MRI arthrogram of the shoulder showing a Bankart lesion with contrast tracking into the anteroinferior labrum
images.caption: MRI arthrogram demonstrating a Bankart lesion — the anteroinferior labral detachment that is the hallmark of traumatic anterior shoulder instability.

images.altText: Arthroscopic view of a shoulder repair procedure with suture anchors visible in the glenoid
images.caption: Arthroscopic view of a Bankart repair — suture anchors are placed at the glenoid rim to re-attach the detached labrum.
